content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jane Ogle, Deputy Director 
CA Department of Health Care Services 
PO Box 2197 
Los Gatos, CA 95031 
March 17, 2014 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-14-023 
Dear Ms. Ogle: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter should not be construed as assistance on any 
conduct that may have already taken place. (See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) In addition, this 
letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the 
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 
I FPPC Ops. 71.) Because rour questions are general in nature, we are treating your request as 
one for informal assistance.· 
We offer no opinion on the application of any laws other than the Political Reform Act 
and Government Code Section 1090 that may apply, such as the post-employment provisions of 
Public Contract Code Section 1041 l. 
While the Commission may provide assistance regarding Government Code Section 
1090, this section applies only if you participated in making the contract. As stated in Millbrae 
Assn. for Residential Sun•ival v. City of Millbrae ( 1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237: 
"Although section 1090 refers to a contract 'made' by the officer or 
employee, the word 'made' is not used in the statute in its narrower and technical 
contract sense but is used in the broad sense to encompass such embodiments in 
the making of a contract as preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
rererences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are lo Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does nol provide the requcstor wilh the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) 


File No. 1-14-023 
Page No. 2 
reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications and solicitation for bids. 
[Citation.] Such construction is predicated upon the rationale that government 
officers and employees are expected to exercise absolute loyalty and undivided 
allegiance to the best interests of the governmental body or agency of which they 
are officers or employees, and upon the basis that the object of such a statute is to 
remove or limit the possibility of any personal influence, either directly or 
indirectly which may bear on an officer's or employee's decision. [Citation.]" 
You stated that you had no involvement with the management of your former agency's 
contract with Harbage Consulting so it does not appear that Section 1090 is at issue. If, however, 
you may have advised or consulted any other agency employee (such as the agency's contract 
manager) regarding any aspect of the contract, you may wish to seek additional advice regarding 
Section 1090 describing any involvement you may have had in the contracting process. 
QUESTIONS 
Upon your retirement from state service, may you assist ( 1) Harbage Consulting with the 
management of a project that you were responsible for as a state official, (2) Harbage Consulting 
with similar projects outside of the state, or (3) other organizations with projects before your 
former agency? 
CONCLUSIONS 
The one-year ban does not prohibit your employment with Harbage Consulting or any 
other organization. However, the ban does prohibit you from making any appearance or 
communication for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, 
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property for 12 months after 
leaving state employment. While you may not be precluded from assisting Harbage Consulting 
or any other organization with project management, your actions on behalf of your new 
employer will be extensively restricted as you may not contact your former agency in regard to 
general decisions involving the Coordinated Care Initiative or any other administrative action. 
Similarly, the permanent ban does not prohibit your employment with Harbage 
Consulting or any other organization. However, the permanent ban does apply to certain 
proceedings in which you previously participated. For purposes of this analysis, the only 
proceeding you have identified is the performance of the contract between Harbage Consulting 
and your former agency. Because you have previously participated in the performance of this 
contract, you may not aid, advise, counsel, consult with or in any way assist Harbage Consulting 
or communicate with your former agency for the purpose of influencing: (i) the amendment or 
revocation of the existing contract; (ii) the issuance or awarding of a substantially similar 
contract; or (iii) agency decisions that, although still within the contract's terms, are likely to 
result in more than a de minimis change in the level of services or goods provided by the 
contractor from that originally contemplated by the agency. 


File No. 1-14-023 
Page No. 3 
Neither the one-year ban nor the permanent ban prohibits you from assisting Harbage 
Consulting on similar projects in states other than California. 
FACTS 
You retired from your position as the Deputy Director of Health Care Delivery Systems 
at the Department of Health Care Services (the "DHCS") at the end of February. In this position, 
you had primary responsibility for a large project called the Coordinated Care Initiative (the 
"CCI project"). Before your employment with the DHCS, the department had contracted with a 
private consulting firm, Harbage Consulting, to provide technical support, project management 
and outreach activities for the CCI project. You have never participated in the management of 
that contract and state that you have never participated in the negotiation or terms of the 
contracts or signed any contract invoices. An employee within your division manages the 
contract, but does not report to you. 
Upon your retirement, you wish to continue to work on the CCI project and would like to 
publicly support the project and assist Harbage Consulting with project management on a part­
time basis. Additionally, you ask whether you can work for (I) Harbage Consulting on similar 
projects in other states that have no ties to California and/or (2) other organizations on projects 
before your former agency. 
ANALYSIS 
Under the Act, public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post­
governmental employment provisions known as the permanent and one-year bans. In addition, 
Section 87407 prohibits certain state and local officials from making, participating in making, or 
using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are 
negotiating employment or have any arrangement concerning employment. (See Regulation 
18747.)3 Colloquially, these provisions are known as the "revolving door" prohibitions. 
One-Year Ban 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, 
any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or 
her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action4 or any 
3 Because you have are already left the Department of Health Care Services, we are not providing advice 
pertaining to Section 87407 at this time. 
4 For purposes of Section 87406, the Act defines "administrative action" and "legislative action" as the 
following: 
"'Administrative action' means the proposal, drafting, development, consideration, 
amendment, enactment, or defeat by any stale agency of any rule, regulation, or other action in 
any ralemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding .... " (Section 82002(a).) 


File No. 1-14-023 
Page No. 4 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, 
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; 
Regulation 18746.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
(Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746.l(a)(2).)5 The ban applies for twelve months from the 
date the employee permanently leaves state office or employment.6 While in effect, the one-year 
ban applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her 
appearances or communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an 
agent, attorney, or representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.1 (b)(3) and (4).) 
In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to "judicial or quasi-judicial" 
proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the 
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or 
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 18746.1 (b )(5).) An 
appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the "principal 
purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the 
action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication includes 
conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission, 
attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (/d.) 
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited if they are ( 1) before a state 
agency that the public official worked for or represented or (2) before a state agency "which 
budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public 
official worked for or represented. (Regulation 187 46.1 (b )(6).) 
As the Deputy Director, your position is or should be designated in the DHCS's conflict­
of-interest code, and your post-employment actions as a paid consultant are restricted under the 
'"Legislative action' means the drafting, introduction, consideration. modification, 
enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the 
Legislature or by either house or any comminee, subcommittee, joint or select commiuee thereof, 
or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. 'Legislative action· 
also means the action of the Governor in approving or vetoing any bill." (Section 82037.) 
5 A governmental employee should be designated in his or her agency's conflict-of-interesl code if the 
employee makes or participates in making governmental decisions that have a reasonably foreseeable material effect 
on any financial interest. (Section 87302.) 
6 For purposes of the one-year ban, the date on which an official permanently leaves office or employment 
is the date on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the duties of that office or employment, and slops 
performing those duties. A person shall not be deemed to have left office permanently because he or she is on a 
leave of absence or serves as an intermittent employee. However, a person shall be deemed 10 have left office 
permanently if the person merely receives compensation for accrued leave credilS. (Regulation 18746.4(b).) 


File No. 1-14-023 
Page No. 5 
one-year ban for 12 months from the date you permanently leave the department. As addressed 
above, the one-year ban applies to any administrative or legislative action and any action or 
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. Accordingly, the one-year ban 
prohibits you from representing Harbage Consulting or any other organization in administrative 
proceedings before the Department of Health Care Services, such as general decisions regarding 
the CCI project, and in any proceedings involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or 
revocation of the a contract. 
Pen11ane11tBa11 
The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in any specific proceeding involving the State of California or 
assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state employee 
participated while employed by the state. (Sections 8740 l and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) The 
permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of absence from any 
particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741.1 (a)( 1 ).)7 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or 
any oral or written communication, or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication, made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other 
proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
'"Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency ... . " (Section 87400(c).) An official is considered to have "participated" 
in a proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through 
decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a 
substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information ... . " (Section 87400(d).) 
"The permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
A 'new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, a 
different subject matter, or different factual issues from those considered in previous 
proceedings." (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; also see Donovan Advice Letter, No. 1-03-
119.) New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not 
participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. 1-89-649.) A new 
contract is one that is based on new consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same 
parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. 1-99-104; Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In 
7 For purposes of the permanent ban, "[t]he date on which an official permanently leaves office or 
employment or takes a leave of absence is the date on which the official is no longer authorized to perform the 
duties of the office or employment, and the official stops performing those duties, even if the official continues to 
receive compensation for accrued leave credits," (Regulation J 8746.4(a)( I).) 


File No. 1-14-023 
Page No. 6 
addition, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered 
to be a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or 
approval. (Anderson, supra; Blonien Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.) 
As the former Deputy Director for the DHCS, you are a former state employee subject to 
the permanent ban. Accordingly, you are prohibited from making an appearance or 
communication, or from assisting any other person in making an appearance or communication, 
before any state administrative agency, for compensation, in a quasi-judicial proceeding if you 
previously participated in the proceeding as a state official. However, under the facts presented, 
the only issue involving the Act's permanent ban is whether your responsibility over the program 
constituted "participation in a proceeding" and, if so, what restrictions would apply upon leaving 
your state position. 
We have advised that the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract is considered 
to be a proceeding separate from the monitoring, performance or implementation of the contract 
(hereafter "performance phase"). (Blo11ie11 Advice Letter, supra and Kernan Advice Letter, No. 
1-12-017.) While you have stated that you did not make or participate in the management or 
negotiations of the Harbage Consulting contract, you did have primary responsibility over the 
CCI program and, therefore, oversight over the performance of the contact. Thus, it appears that 
your participation in the performance phase of the contract was "personal and substantial" and 
considered a proceeding to which the permanently ban will apply.8 
Nonetheless, we have previously advised that the permanent ban does not prohibit a state 
employee who worked on the performance phase of an agency contract from working on the 
same phase of the contract for a private contractor after leaving state service. The permanent ban 
only restricts a former employee from assisting the contractor for the purpose of influencing a 
decision with a financial effect on the contractor. As explained in the de/ Valle Advice Letter, 
No. A-12-086, a former employee who worked on the performance phase of a contract cannot, as 
part of his work for the contractor or any other non•state entity, aid, advise, counsel, consult 
with or in any way assist the contractor or communicate with his or her former state agency's 
staff or representatives for the purpose of influencing: (i) the amendment or revocation of the 
existing contract; (ii) the issuance or awarding of a substantially similar contract; or (iii) agency 
decisions that, although still within the contract's terms, are likely to result in more than a de 
minim is change in the level of services or goods provided by the contractor from that originally 
contemplated by the agency. 
You have specifically asked about assisting Harbage Consulting with ongoing project 
management. Generally, so long as your activities fall within the existing terms of the Harbage 
Consulting contract, merely managing the project would not be prohibited under the permanent 
8 "A supervisor is deemed to have participated in any proceeding that was 'pending before' ... the 
official's agency and that was under his or her supervisory authority." A proceeding is under a supervisor's 
"authority" if the supervisor duties "include primary responsibility within the agency for directing the operation or 
function of the program where the proceeding is initiated or conducted." (Regulation 18741.1.) 


File No. 1-14-023 
Page No. 7 
ban. However, we caution that you should seek additional advice if your activities may affect 
the existing contractual terms. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Counsel, Legal Division

former agency?

Neither the one-year ban nor the permanent ban prohibits you from assisting Harbage 
Consulting on similar projects in states other than California.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Upon your retirement from state service, may you assist ( 1) Harbage Consulting with the 
management of a project that you were responsible for as a state official, (2) Harbage Consulting 
with similar projects outside of the state, or (3) other organizations with projects before your 
former agency?

CONCLUSION: The one-year ban does not prohibit your employment with Harbage Consulting or any 
other organization. However, the ban does prohibit you from making any appearance or 
communication for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative action or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, 
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property for 12 months after 
leaving state employment. While you may not be precluded from assisting Harbage Consulting 
or any other organization with project management, your actions on behalf of your new 
employer will be extensively restricted as you may not contact your former agency in regard to 
general decisions involving the Coordinated Care Initiative or any other administrative action. 
Similarly, the permanent ban does not prohibit your employment with Harbage 
Consulting or any other organization. However, the permanent ban does apply to certain 
proceedings in which you previously participated. For purposes of this analysis, the only 
proceeding you have identified is the performance of the contract between Harbage Consulting 
and your former agency. Because you have previously participated in the performance of this 
contract, you may not aid, advise, counsel, consult with or in any way assist Harbage Consulting 
or communicate with your former agency for the purpose of influencing: (i) the amendment or 
revocation of the existing contract; (ii) the issuance or awarding of a substantially similar 
contract; or (iii) agency decisions that, although still within the contract's terms, are likely to 
result in more than a de minimis change in the level of services or goods provided by the 
contractor from that originally contemplated by the agency.  
Neither the one-year ban nor the permanent ban prohibits you from assisting Harbage 
Consulting on similar projects in states other than California.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Jane Ogle, Deputy Director CA Department of Health Care Services PO Box 2197 Los Gatos, CA 95031 March 17, 2014 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1-14-023 Dear Ms. Ogle: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place. (See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Because rour questions are general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.· We offer no opinion on the application of any laws other than the Political Reform Act and Government Code Section 1090 that may apply, such as the post-employment provisions of Public Contract Code Section 1041 l. While the Commission may provide assistance regarding Government Code Section 1090, this section applies only if you participated in making the contract. As stated in Millbrae Assn. for Residential Sun•ival v. City of Millbrae ( 1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237: "Although section 1090 refers to a contract 'made' by the officer or employee, the word 'made' is not used in the statute in its narrower and technical contract sense but is used in the broad sense to encompass such embodiments in the making of a contract as preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory rererences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are lo Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 2 Informal assistance does nol provide the requcstor wilh the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) File No. 1-14-023 Page No. 2 reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications and solicitation for bids. [Citation.] Such construction is predicated upon the rationale that government officers and employees are expected to exercise absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance to the best interests of the governmental body or agency of which they are officers or employees, and upon the basis that the object of such a statute is to remove or limit the possibility of any personal influence, either directly or indirectly which may bear on an officer's or employee's decision. [Citation.]" You stated that you had no involvement with the management of your former agency's contract with Harbage Consulting so it does not appear that Section 1090 is at issue. If, however, you may have advised or consulted any other agency employee (such
